slides.title: The Policies That Protect People and Workstations
slides.subtitle: Protecting Spaces: the managerial controls — security awareness training and a workstation security policy — that the College Board says protect a physical workplace
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Lesson 2.3, Protecting Spaces, Day 1 of 4. In Topic 2.2 you learned to find physical vulnerabilities and assess the risk they create. Topic 2.3 is the answer to that risk: the controls that protect a physical space. We have four class periods. Today, Day 1, is learning objective 2.3.A — managerial controls, which are the policies and people-focused measures that protect a workplace. Days 2 and 3 are 2.3.B, the physical controls and how to choose them, with Day 3 applying them to Scenario 2A, Securing Xtensr Labs. Day 4 is review and practice. Today, two things: employee security awareness training, EK 2.3.A.1, and the workstation security policy, EK 2.3.A.2. Notice the word managerial — these are not fences and locks, they are rules and training. That distinction is exactly what the exam tests in 2.3.A.

slides.prompt: You badge into your office's restricted lab. As the door swings shut behind you, a person carrying a laptop bag and a coffee smiles and says, ‘Hey, can you hold that? I left my badge at my desk.’ At the same time, a coworker has just walked away from their unlocked computer with a printed client list still on the desk.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: name two things that just went wrong here, and for each, describe a rule or a habit — not a fence or a lock — that would have prevented it.
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own, no notes. Read it: you badge into a restricted lab, and a friendly stranger asks you to hold the door because they ‘forgot their badge’ — and meanwhile a coworker walked off leaving their computer unlocked with a client list sitting on the desk. In three to five sentences, name two things that just went wrong, and for each, give me a rule or a habit that would have stopped it — not a fence, not a lock, a rule or a habit. Hold your answer. Both problems here are solved by managerial controls — training and policy — and that is exactly today's learning objective, 2.3.A.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 1 Guided Notes have three sections that match today’s lesson. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: Managerial Controls: Training People
sections.description: Security awareness training teaches employees to detect phishing, not badge others into restricted areas, and prevent device theft (EK 2.3.A.1).

sections.title: Managerial Controls: Workstation Policy
sections.description: A workstation security policy outlines measures to protect a workplace — locking devices, clear-desk, privacy screens, and surge protection (EK 2.3.A.2).

sections.title: Enrichment: Managerial vs. Physical Controls
sections.description: Today's controls are managerial (rules and training); Days 2–3 cover physical controls (fences, locks, card readers). Knowing the category is an exam skill.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.3 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Three sections today, two green and one blue. Section one is the first managerial control, security awareness training — teaching people to spot phishing, to stop tailgating, and to prevent device theft. Section two is the second managerial control, the workstation security policy, with its four common requirements. Section three is a short enrichment that names the bigger picture: today is managerial controls, rules and training, while Days 2 and 3 are physical controls, fences and locks. Telling those two categories apart is a tested skill, so I want it in your notes. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end of class.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Identify the two managerial controls in EK 2.3.A and the three behaviors security awareness training targets — phishing, tailgating, device theft (LO 2.3.A)
Explain what a workstation security policy requires — locking devices, a clean desk, a privacy screen, surge/UPS — and what tiers means (LO 2.3.A)
Distinguish a managerial control from a physical control when a measure is described (LO 2.3.A)

slides.script: Three objectives today, all under learning objective 2.3.A — identify managerial controls related to physical security. First, identify the two managerial controls the College Board names — security awareness training and the workstation security policy — and the three behaviors training targets: detecting phishing, not badging others in, and preventing device theft. Second, explain what a workstation security policy requires — locking devices, a clean desk, a privacy screen filter, a surge protector or UPS — and what the word tiers means. Third, distinguish a managerial control from a physical control when a measure is described, because the exam loves to disguise a policy or a training program as if it were a lock or a gate. By the end of today you can do all three.

slides.label: Security Awareness Training
slides.script: Section one. Learning objective 2.3.A, the first of two managerial controls: employee security awareness training. The idea is simple but powerful — your people are a control. Ten minutes here.

slides.heading: Security Awareness Training (EK 2.3.A.1)
slides.subheading: Organizations train employees to contribute to security. The CED names three behaviors training targets.

left.title: What Training Teaches
left.definition: Educate employees on how they contribute to security
examples: Detecting social engineering attempts like phishing
Not badging other people into restricted areas
The employee becomes an active part of the organization's defense

right.title: Why It’s a Managerial Control
right.definition: It changes behavior with policy and education, not hardware
examples: No lock or fence stops a tailgater the way a trained employee does
Phishing bypasses technical controls by targeting the person
Device theft is prevented by habits, not just hardware
Managerial = rules, training, and policy that direct people

slides.footer: AP TIP: EK 2.3.A.1 names exactly three behaviors security awareness training addresses — phishing detection, not badging others in (tailgating), and preventing device theft. Memorize all three by name.
slides.script: Here is the first managerial control, EK 2.3.A.1: organizations should conduct employee security awareness training to educate employees about how they can contribute to the organization's security. The College Board names three specific behaviors. One, detecting social engineering attempts like phishing — the employee learns to spot the trick email. Two, not badging other people into restricted areas — that is the door-holding stranger from your bell ringer, called tailgating, and the trained employee politely refuses. Three, preventing device theft — habits like never leaving a laptop unattended. On the right, why this counts as managerial: no fence or lock catches a tailgater the way a trained person does, and phishing slips past technical controls by targeting the human. Managerial controls direct people through rules and training. Write EK 2.3.A.1 and all three behaviors.

slides.label: Workstation Security Policy
slides.script: Section two. Same objective, 2.3.A, the second managerial control: the workstation security policy. This is the rulebook for what happens at and around an employee's desk. Ten minutes.

slides.heading: The Workstation Security Policy (EK 2.3.A.2)
slides.subheading: A policy outlining measures to protect a workplace. It may have TIERS based on the sensitivity of data handled. Three of its common requirements:

items.title: Lock and Clear the Desk
items.summary: Locking devices before leaving workstations unattended prevents unauthorized access, and clearing sensitive documents off workstations before leaving them unattended — sometimes called a clean desk policy — keeps printed information from being read or taken.

items.title: Block the Sightline
items.summary: Using a privacy screen filter or other physical barrier prevents others from viewing information on the screen — defeating shoulder-surfing in shared or public spaces where an unauthorized person could read sensitive data over an employee's shoulder.

items.title: Protect the Power
items.summary: Connecting devices to surge protectors or uninterruptible power supplies (UPS) protects equipment and data from power problems, keeping a workstation available and preventing data loss when power spikes or fails.

slides.script: The second managerial control, EK 2.3.A.2: organizations should have a workstation security policy that outlines the measures necessary to protect a physical workplace. Two things to note before the list. First, the policy may have tiers of workstation security based on the type of data handled at a workstation — a desk handling medical records gets stricter rules than a lobby kiosk. Second, here are the common requirements. Lock devices before leaving them unattended to prevent unauthorized access, and clear sensitive documents off the desk — that is the clean desk policy. Use a privacy screen filter or other physical barrier so no one can read the screen over your shoulder. And connect devices to surge protectors or an uninterruptible power supply, a UPS, to protect against power problems. Four requirements, one policy. Write EK 2.3.A.2 and the requirements, and write the word tiers.

slides.heading: The ‘Only Locks and Fences Count’ Myth
slides.misconception: Physical security means physical objects — locks, fences, cameras. Training and policies are paperwork, not real security controls.
slides.whyItHappens: ‘Physical security’ sounds like it should involve physical hardware, so training and written policy feel like soft, secondary measures rather than genuine controls that protect a space.
slides.correction: EK 2.3.A is explicitly about MANAGERIAL controls related to physical security: security awareness training (A.1) and a workstation security policy (A.2). These ARE security controls — they direct people's behavior, which is what stops tailgating, phishing, device theft, and shoulder-surfing. The CED puts them first in Topic 2.3, before any fence or lock.
slides.example: A building can have the best card readers money can buy, and one untrained employee holding the door for a stranger defeats them instantly. Conversely, a clean desk policy and locked screens stop an intruder who is already inside from reading or photographing sensitive data — something no perimeter fence can do. Managerial controls cover the gaps hardware can't.
slides.script: Here is the misconception built into how 2.3.A is worded. The phrase ‘physical security’ makes students assume the controls must be physical objects — locks, fences, cameras — and that training and policy are just paperwork. But read the objective: it says identify MANAGERIAL controls related to physical security. Managerial means the rules and training that direct people, and the College Board lists them first in Topic 2.3, before any hardware. Why do they matter so much? Picture the best card readers money can buy, and one untrained employee holds the door for a stranger — defeated instantly. The other direction: a clean desk policy and a locked screen stop someone already inside from reading sensitive data, which no fence can do. So when a question describes training or a desk policy, the category is managerial. Do not let the word ‘physical’ trick you into calling it a physical control.

slides.heading: Enrichment: Managerial vs. Physical Controls
slides.subheading: Not a new EK, but a sorting skill the exam rewards: today is managerial; Days 2–3 are physical.

left.title: Managerial Controls (Today, 2.3.A)
left.definition: Rules, policies, and training that direct people
examples: Security awareness training: stop phishing, tailgating, theft (EK 2.3.A.1)
Workstation security policy: lock, clear desk, privacy screen, UPS (EK 2.3.A.2)
The control is a decision a person makes or a habit they keep
Defeated by an untrained or careless human, not a cut wire

right.title: Physical Controls (Days 2–3, 2.3.B)
right.definition: Hardware and barriers that physically deter or prevent
examples: Fencing, gates, bollards deter access; locks prevent theft (EK 2.3.B.2–B.3)
Card readers, vestibules, turnstiles control who gets in (EK 2.3.B.4–B.5)
Disabled USB ports, UPS, and generators protect devices (EK 2.3.B.6–B.7)
The control is a physical object doing its job whether or not anyone is trained

slides.footer: TAKEAWAY: Both are ‘physical security,’ but the CED splits the CONTROLS into managerial (2.3.A: people and policy) and physical (2.3.B: hardware). A strong answer names the category, then the specific control.
slides.script: Quick enrichment, because the sorting is worth points. On the left, today's managerial controls: security awareness training and the workstation security policy. The thing they have in common is that the control is a decision a person makes or a habit they keep — and they're defeated by a careless human, not a cut wire. On the right, the physical controls coming in Days 2 and 3 under 2.3.B: fences, gates, bollards, locks, card readers, vestibules, turnstiles, disabled USB ports, UPS and generators. Those are objects that do their job whether or not anyone was trained. Both halves are ‘physical security,’ but the College Board splits the controls into managerial — people and policy — and physical — hardware. On the exam, name the category first, then the specific control. That two-part answer is what scores.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 2.3.A)

terms.definition: A security control that protects a space through rules, policy, and training rather than hardware, such as security awareness training or a workstation policy (EK 2.3.A.1).
Training that educates employees on how they contribute to security — detecting phishing, not badging others into restricted areas, and preventing device theft (EK 2.3.A.1).
Following an authorized person into a restricted area without badging in; security awareness training teaches employees not to badge or hold the door for others (EK 2.3.A.1).
A policy outlining the measures necessary to protect a physical workplace, which may have tiers based on the type of data handled (EK 2.3.A.2).
The requirement to clear sensitive documents off a workstation before leaving it unattended, so printed information cannot be read or taken (EK 2.3.A.2).
A physical barrier on a screen that prevents others from viewing the information on it, defeating shoulder-surfing (EK 2.3.A.2).

slides.script: Six terms, anchored on the two EKs. The big organizing word is managerial control — a control made of rules and training, not hardware. Under EK 2.3.A.1 know security awareness training and the behavior it stops, tailgating, which is following or being let into a restricted area without badging. Under EK 2.3.A.2 know the workstation security policy and two of its named pieces: the clean desk policy, clearing sensitive documents, and the privacy screen filter, which blocks shoulder-surfing. Know all six by name and EK number — and for each, be ready to say it's a managerial control.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 2.3.A. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: An employee receives an email that looks like it's from IT asking them to ‘confirm your badge number,’ and later a stranger asks them to hold a restricted door. Name the single managerial control that addresses BOTH situations, cite its EK number, and list the three behaviors the CED says it targets.

A hospital wants stricter desk rules for workstations that handle patient records than for the front-lobby check-in kiosk. Name the managerial control involved, cite its EK, explain the term ‘tiers,’ and list two of the workstation requirements the CED names.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: a phishing email plus a door-holding stranger — both are solved by one managerial control, security awareness training, EK 2.3.A.1, and I want all three behaviors it targets: phishing detection, not badging others in, and preventing device theft. Question two: a hospital wanting stricter rules for record-handling desks than for the lobby kiosk — that's the workstation security policy, EK 2.3.A.2, the idea is tiers based on the type of data handled, and give me two named requirements like locking devices or the clean desk policy. Be precise and cite the EK.

slides.eyebrow: Your Turn — It's All on the Site
slides.heading: Your Turn

items.label: Lesson 2.3 page
items.tail: Read the lesson and run the self-check.

items.label: Exercise 1
items.tail: Sort each measure: managerial or physical control?

items.label: Exercise 2
items.tail: Match the workplace risk to its managerial control.

slides.note: The lesson video covers this exact material, slide for slide.
slides.script: Everything from class is on the site, and work it in order. Start on the Lesson 2.3 page and run the self-check. Then Exercise 1, where you sort each measure into managerial or physical — the skill from our enrichment — and Exercise 2, where you match each workplace risk to the managerial control that addresses it. The quiz at the bottom is five questions across both objectives; you'll be ready for all of it by Day 4. Write the lesson page URL down now.

slides.heading: End of Day 1
todayPoints: Identified the two managerial controls in EK 2.3.A: security awareness training (A.1) and a workstation security policy (A.2)
Named the three behaviors training targets: detecting phishing, not badging others in (tailgating), and preventing device theft (A.1)
Named the workstation policy requirements: lock devices, clean desk, privacy screen filter, surge protector/UPS — and the idea of tiers (A.2)
Learned to sort a control as managerial (rules and training) vs. physical (hardware) — a tested skill
nextPoints: Move from managerial controls to physical controls and mitigation strategies (LO 2.3.B)
See how a defender chooses a control by thinking like an adversary: prevent, detect, or correct
Begin building the toolkit you'll apply to Scenario 2A on Day 3
slides.teaser: Tonight, picture the outside of a secure building — the fence, the gate, the badge reader at the door, the locked server cabinet inside. Tomorrow you start naming each one and learning exactly which vulnerability it mitigates.
slides.script: Day 1 done. You can identify both managerial controls — security awareness training and the workstation security policy — and you can tell a managerial control from a physical one, which is the trap the exam sets in 2.3.A. Tomorrow we cross into 2.3.B and the physical controls: how a defender reasons like an adversary to pick a control that prevents, detects, or corrects an attack, and the specific hardware the College Board names — fences, locks, card readers, vestibules, and more. That toolkit is what you'll apply to Scenario 2A, Securing Xtensr Labs, on Day 3. Review your Guided Notes — be able to name both managerial controls, their EK numbers, and every behavior and requirement under each.

quiz.title: Lesson 2.3 — Protecting Spaces — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 25 minutes, no notes. Cite EK numbers in any written justification your teacher requires.

questions.stem: At Xtensr Labs, a security technician proposes four measures for the new building. Which one is a MANAGERIAL control related to physical security, as described in EK 2.3.A?
options: Installing bollards in front of the lobby entrance to stop vehicles
Adding a card reader to the restricted server room door
Building an access control vestibule at the main entrance
Requiring all employees to complete security awareness training on phishing, tailgating, and device theft
questions.rationale: EK 2.3.A.1 names employee security awareness training as a managerial control — it protects the space by educating people on detecting phishing, not badging others into restricted areas, and preventing device theft. Options A, B, and C (bollards, card readers, access control vestibules) are physical controls from EK 2.3.B, not managerial controls. The question turns on the category, not just whether the measure helps security.

questions.stem: An Xtensr employee badges into a restricted lab. A person carrying boxes asks the employee to hold the door because they ‘forgot their badge.’ Per the CED, which managerial control is specifically intended to address this situation?
options: A workstation security policy requiring employees to keep a clean desk
Security awareness training, which teaches employees not to badge others in
A surge protector connected to each workstation to guard against power loss
A privacy screen filter installed on every employee monitor in the lab
questions.rationale: EK 2.3.A.1 lists ‘not badging other people into restricted areas’ as one of the three behaviors security awareness training targets — this is tailgating, exactly the situation described. Option A (clean desk) and Option D (privacy screen) are workstation-policy requirements under EK 2.3.A.2 that address desk-level data exposure, not door access. Option C (surge protector) addresses power problems, not unauthorized entry.

questions.stem: A hospital writes its workstation security policy so that desks handling patient records follow stricter rules than the front-lobby check-in kiosk. Which feature of a workstation security policy, as described in EK 2.3.A.2, does this represent?
options: Tiers of workstation security based on the type of data handled at a workstation
Disabling USB ports to prevent malware from external drives
Installing fencing and gates around the building perimeter
Conducting annual phishing-detection drills for all staff
questions.rationale: EK 2.3.A.2 states that a workstation security policy may have tiers of workstation security based on the type of data handled at a workstation — stricter rules for record-handling desks is exactly that tiering. Option B (disabling USB ports) is a physical control under EK 2.3.B.6. Option C (fencing/gates) is a physical deterrent under EK 2.3.B.2. Option D (phishing drills) is part of security awareness training under EK 2.3.A.1, a different managerial control.

questions.stem: A workstation security policy is being drafted. Which of the following are requirements the CED says workstation policies often include? I. Locking devices before leaving workstations unattended. II. Clearing sensitive documents off workstations before leaving them unattended. III. Connecting devices to surge protectors or an uninterruptible power supply (UPS).
options: I only
II and III only
I, II, and III
I and II only
questions.rationale: EK 2.3.A.2 lists all three as requirements workstation policies often include: locking devices before leaving them unattended to prevent unauthorized access (I), clearing sensitive documents — the clean desk policy (II), and connecting devices to surge protectors or a UPS (III). It also names using a privacy screen filter. All three statements are CED-named workstation-policy requirements, so the answer is I, II, and III.

questions.stem: An Xtensr analyst frequently works on sensitive data from a shared open-plan desk near a busy walkway, and twice has noticed someone glancing at the screen while passing. Which workstation-policy measure from EK 2.3.A.2 most directly addresses this risk?
options: A surge protector or uninterruptible power supply (UPS) on the workstation
Security awareness training about preventing device theft
Bollards placed along the walkway outside the building
A privacy screen filter or other physical barrier to prevent others from viewing the screen
questions.rationale: EK 2.3.A.2 names using a privacy screen filter or other physical barrier to prevent others from viewing information on the screen — this directly addresses passersby reading a screen, known as shoulder-surfing. Option A (surge protector/UPS) addresses power problems, not screen viewing. Option B is a managerial control but targets device theft, not on-screen data exposure. Option C (bollards) is a physical perimeter control from EK 2.3.B.2 and does nothing about an indoor sightline.

guide.title: Lesson 2.3 — Protecting Spaces — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Unit 2 · Topic 2.3 · 4 class periods · Scenario 2A: Securing Xtensr Labs
overview: Lesson 2.3 is the ‘protect’ half of Unit 2: having identified physical vulnerabilities and assessed their risk in Topic 2.2, students now choose controls to mitigate them. Across four days students learn to identify the managerial controls related to physical security (EK 2.3.A.1–A.2) and to determine mitigation strategies — physical controls — for risks from physical vulnerabilities (EK 2.3.B.1–B.8). Day 1's organizing idea is the managerial/physical distinction: 2.3.A controls are rules and training (security awareness training and a workstation security policy) that direct people, not hardware. Days 2–3 build the physical-control toolkit and the prevent/detect/correct reasoning of EK 2.3.B.1, with Day 3 applying it to Scenario 2A. Day 4 consolidates both objectives with worked practice and a cumulative scenario.

Scenario 2A (Securing Xtensr Labs) anchors the lesson: the student works on the physical security team at Xtensr Research Labs, which is acquiring a smaller research company in town, and must conduct a physical vulnerability assessment of the new lab — reviewing building plans and current controls to identify vulnerabilities, assessing their risk, recommending physical security controls to mitigate them, and recommending placement of monitoring equipment. Topic 2.3's slice of 2A is recommending physical security controls to mitigate the risks identified in Topic 2.2; the detection/placement slice belongs to Topic 2.4. Naming note: the CB scenario names the company (Xtensr Research Labs) but no individuals; none is added here.
los: LO 2.3.A — Identify managerial controls related to physical security.
LO 2.3.B — Determine mitigation strategies for risks from physical vulnerabilities.
eks: EK 2.3.A.1 — Organizations should conduct employee security awareness training to educate employees about how they can contribute to the organization's security by: detecting social engineering attempts like phishing; not badging other people into restricted areas; preventing device theft.

EK 2.3.A.2 — Organizations should have a workstation security policy that outlines the measures necessary to protect a physical workplace. The policy may have tiers of workstation security based on the type of data handled at a workstation. Workstation policies often require: locking devices before leaving workstations unattended to prevent unauthorized access; clearing sensitive documents off workstations before leaving them unattended (sometimes called a clean desk policy); using a privacy screen filter or other physical barrier to prevent others from viewing information on the screen; connecting devices to surge protectors or uninterruptible power supplies (UPS).
EK 2.3.B.1 — To determine a relevant control, a cyber defender considers how an adversary could take advantage of a vulnerability to attack a system and how to prevent, detect, or correct the attack.
EK 2.3.B.2 — Installing physical controls like fencing, gates, and bollards around a building can deter adversaries from trying to physically access an organization's buildings.
EK 2.3.B.3 — Locks on doors, server cabinets, and computers can prevent devices from being accessed or stolen.
EK 2.3.B.4 — Card readers can record which employee badges are being used to access different entries at specific times and deny access to unauthorized badges.
EK 2.3.B.5 — Access control vestibules and turnstiles can prevent an authorized person from intentionally or accidentally admitting an unauthorized person into a restricted area.
EK 2.3.B.6 — Organizations can disable USB ports to prevent external drives from loading malware onto a computer.

EK 2.3.B.7 — An uninterruptible power supply (UPS) provides a backup power source for a device in the event of a power outage. Organizations can also use power generators to provide power at a larger scale to a building or set of critical devices.
EK 2.3.B.8 — Organizations prioritize risk mitigations based on the severity of the risks and the cost of the recommended mitigations.
materials: Projector / slide deck (teacher · deep-dive version) for each day.

Printed/Digital Day 1–4 Guided Notes (student copies). Workflow: students fill the notes in live during the slide presentation; anything missed can be completed — or checked — afterward against the lesson page (apcsexamprep.com/pages/ap-cybersecurity-unit-2-protecting-spaces), which carries all the content. The formal answer KEYs stay in this folder and are never posted to the site.
Printed Lesson 2.3 quiz (student copies) for Day 4.
Scenario 2A narrative (Securing Xtensr Labs) displayed on the Day 3 and Day 4 decks (no handout needed).
prereqs: Topic 2.2 (Physical Vulnerabilities and Attacks). Lesson 2.3 mitigates the vulnerabilities students identified and the risks they assessed in 2.2 — EK 2.3.B controls directly answer the risks from physical vulnerabilities, and the Topic 2.3 slice of Scenario 2A is recommending controls to mitigate the risks identified in Topic 2.2.

misconceptions.heading: The ‘only locks and fences count’ myth
misconceptions.myth: Physical security means physical objects, so training and written policies are not real security controls.
misconceptions.reality: EK 2.3.A names two managerial controls — security awareness training (A.1) and a workstation security policy (A.2) — that protect a space by directing people's behavior. They stop tailgating, phishing, device theft, and shoulder-surfing, which hardware alone cannot.

misconceptions.heading: Managerial vs. physical control confusion
misconceptions.myth: A privacy screen and a UPS are hardware, so they must be physical controls, not part of a managerial control.
misconceptions.reality: A privacy screen filter and a surge protector/UPS are named inside the workstation security POLICY (EK 2.3.A.2), so in this context they are requirements of a managerial control. Category is set by whether the control is a rule/policy or standalone hardware, and by where the CED files it.

misconceptions.heading: Any control fits any vulnerability
misconceptions.myth: Pick any strong-sounding control and the vulnerability is handled.
misconceptions.reality: EK 2.3.B.1 requires reasoning about how an adversary exploits the specific vulnerability and whether the control will prevent, detect, or correct that attack. A bollard does nothing for shoulder-surfing; a privacy screen does nothing for vehicle ramming. The control must match the vulnerability.

misconceptions.heading: More controls is always better
misconceptions.myth: Recommend every control available; more security is always the right answer.
misconceptions.reality: EK 2.3.B.8 says organizations prioritize mitigations based on the severity of the risks and the cost of the recommended mitigations. A good recommendation weighs how serious the risk is against what the control costs, not just how much hardware can be added.

support: Post a two-column sorter before the Stop and Think — ‘Managerial (rules/training)’ vs. ‘Physical (hardware)’ — and have students drop each EK 2.3 control into a column so they recall the category, not just the control.
Provide a sentence frame: ‘This is a ____ control (EK 2.3.____) because it ____, which prevents/detects/corrects ____.’
extension: Have students take one Topic 2.2 vulnerability and propose both a managerial (2.3.A) and a physical (2.3.B) control for it, then argue which they'd fund first using EK 2.3.B.8 (severity vs. cost).
Ask students to classify each EK 2.3.B control as primarily preventing, detecting, or correcting (per EK 2.3.B.1) and defend any control that could fit more than one category.

discussion: Of the two managerial controls in 2.3.A, which do you think is harder to enforce day-to-day — training people not to tailgate, or a clean desk policy — and why?

EK 2.3.B.1 says a defender thinks about how an adversary would exploit a vulnerability before choosing a control. Why is reasoning from the attacker's side a better way to pick controls than starting from a catalog of hardware?
EK 2.3.B.8 forces a severity-vs-cost trade-off. When is it defensible to leave a known vulnerability unmitigated because the fix costs too much?
guide.answerKeyNote: Full per-question rationales: emit with --doc=quiz --mode=key. The summary answer key below is auto-derived from the live quiz block, so it can never drift from the student questions.
